content:
# Final Review Arbiter
**Producer:** agent:claude/claude-opus-4-6

## Summary

Rejected the sole disputed amendment (fr-17-3303d1eb). Code inspection proves the amendment's core claim is wrong: ManagedChild::wait() uses timer-based polling (try_wait + tokio::time::sleep), not blocking OS I/O. With start_paused=true, all pending futures are timer-driven, making auto-advance work by intentional design rather than fragile Tokio-internal coupling. Reviewer 2's rejection was correct; the planner and Reviewer 1 accepted without verifying the wait() implementation.

## Rulings

- `fr-17-3303d1eb`: Reject
  The amendment's premise is factually incorrect. It claims `active_child.wait()` is 'an OS I/O operation that won't trigger auto-advance,' but inspection of ManagedChild::wait() (process_backend.rs:431-455) reveals a polling loop: `child.try_wait()` (non-blocking) followed by `tokio::time::sleep(CHILD_WAIT_POLL_INTERVAL)` (a 10ms timer sleep). Similarly, `confirm_teardown()` (line 1528-1537) wraps the same polling wait in `tokio::time::timeout()`. All pending futures in this test are timer-based, so `start_paused = true` with auto-advance works correctly by design — not as a fragile coupling to Tokio internals. Reviewer 2 correctly identified this: the ManagedChild was explicitly designed with poll-based waiting (comment at lines 436-437 explains this is intentional to allow lock-free cancellation). Adding a comment based on the amendment's false premise would be misleading. The test is sound as written.
